evidence: QueryExecutor interface has thorough Javadoc distinguishing findCollocations vs executeBcqlQuery vs executeSurfacePattern — a developer reading the interface knows which method to use and why

BlackLabSnippetParser has 4+ methods annotated with informal '/* @Nullable */' comments but zero actual @Nullable annotations (JSR-305 or JetBrains) — the null contract is conveyed only by proximity comment, not by a checkable annotation

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() wraps IOException in IllegalStateException with no @throws declaration; callers expecting to handle file-not-found cannot catch it as IOException — the checked-exception contract is silently converted to unchecked
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() documents throws IllegalStateException and is consistent: always throws when relationType() is empty or PosGroup is OTHER — contract is honest
LongIntHashMap.get() returns 0 for absent keys and documents this clearly; also documents EMPTY_KEY sentinel meaning Long.MIN_VALUE cannot be stored — honest about a sharp edge

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations contract says 'throws IllegalArgumentException if cqlPattern is not in recognized format' and buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution does throw IAE for non-[...] patterns — honored
requireNextArg in Main.java calls System.exit(1) without a @throws or checked exception contract — side-effectful terminal behavior hidden in a utility method named 'require'

CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery Javadoc says 'Full two-phase BCQL query execution' correctly describing the three-phase implementation (comment says three phases inside the method) — minor Javadoc/code description drift
ExplorationResult.buildEdges() and ComparisonResult.buildEdges() both document 'performs list construction on every call' — honest about repeated allocation cost
RelationConfig.getDeprel() returns null when pattern is null or relationType is not DEP; this null return is not declared in Javadoc though callers (SketchHandlers) null-check it

strengths: QueryExecutor interface contract is exceptional — method responsibilities, parameter semantics, throw conditions, and result ordering are all precisely documented
LongIntHashMap contract is honest about the Long.MIN_VALUE sentinel restriction and the 0-for-absent return value
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() throws ISE when the contract cannot be fulfilled — explicit failure rather than silent wrong behavior
HttpApiUtils.requireParam() documents the null-return contract and the caller's responsibility to check — unusual honesty for a Java utility
contract_coherence.issue_character: The primary pattern of contract violations is informal null annotations (comment-only @Nullable) and hidden exception wrapping (IOException → IllegalStateException), both of which create caller confusion about what to expect and handle.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong narrative documentation of contracts but relies on informal comments rather than machine-checkable annotations for null safety. The IOException-to-IllegalStateException wrapping in createDefaultEnglish() is a genuine contract lie (callers cannot handle the failure mode they would expect). The requireNextArg System.exit() side effect is undocumented in the method signature. These are real but moderate issues. Anchoring: 80 = mostly honored contracts with a few soft violations, 70 = recurring pattern of informal or incomplete contracts. This lands at 76.

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser uses /* @Nullable */ comment-annotations that are not checkable at compile time or by null-analysis tools
evidence: extractLemmaFromMatch: '/* @Nullable */ static String extractLemmaFromMatch(...)' — returns null when no lemma found
extractPosFromMatch: '/* @Nullable */' — returns null when tag attributes absent
extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition: '/* @Nullable */' — returns null for out-of-range position
extractHeadword: '/* @Nullable */' — returns null when no lemma= attribute found
At least 5 methods use this pattern; none use @org.jetbrains.annotations.Nullable, @javax.annotation.Nullable, or any real annotation
issues.suggestion: Add a dependency on JSR-305 (com.google.code.findbugs:jsr305) or JetBrains annotations, then replace '/* @Nullable */' comments with '@Nullable' annotations. Alternatively, return Optional<String> for the public surface (extractHeadword is already package-private). This enables null-analysis tools (SpotBugs, IntelliJ) to enforce the contract automatically.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() silently converts IOException to IllegalStateException, hiding the true failure contract from callers
evidence: Method signature: 'public static GrammarConfigLoader createDefaultEnglish()' — no throws clause
Implementation wraps IOException in IllegalStateException: 'throw new IllegalStateException("Failed to load default grammar config: " + path, e)'
A caller expecting to handle a missing config file cannot catch IOException — they must catch RuntimeException/IllegalStateException or let it propagate as an uncaught exception
The primary constructor GrammarConfigLoader(Path) correctly declares throws IOException; this factory breaks that contract
issues.suggestion: Either (a) change createDefaultEnglish() to return Optional<GrammarConfigLoader> and propagate IOException — callers decide how to handle it, or (b) keep the unchecked wrapping but document it explicitly in Javadoc: '@throws IllegalStateException wrapping IOException if the config file is missing or unreadable'. Option (b) is the minimal fix if no callers want to recover from this.

issues.summary: requireNextArg() in Main.java calls System.exit(1) — a terminal side-effect undocumented in the method signature
evidence: private static String requireNextArg(String[] args, int i, String option) — returns String, no throws clause
Method body: 'System.exit(1)' when i >= args.length — terminates JVM as a side-effect
Callers treat this as a simple accessor: 'inputPath = requireNextArg(args, ++i, "--input")' — the JVM-exit side effect is not visible from the call site
issues.suggestion: Add a Javadoc comment: '@throws IllegalArgumentException (via System.exit) when the argument is missing' — or better, throw a checked exception and let the main() catch block handle the message-and-exit pattern. This makes the contract visible to future maintainers.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.getDeprel() can return null but does not document this in Javadoc
evidence: Method returns null in three code paths: 'if (pattern == null || !RelationType.DEP.equals(...)) return null', at end of deprel regex match with fallback, and when id doesn't start with 'dep_'
SketchHandlers.handleDepSketchForType() has: 'if (rel.getDeprel() == null) continue;' — confirming callers must null-check
No @Nullable annotation or @return documentation mentioning the null case
issues.suggestion: Add '@return the deprel string, or {@code null} when the relation is not of type DEP, the pattern is null, or no deprel can be derived from the id' to the getDeprel() Javadoc. Optionally return Optional<String> to make null-avoidance compositional.

root_causes: Java standard library contracts are well-honored, but the project lacks a consistent annotation strategy for null returns — informal comments substituted for actual null-safety infrastructure
likely_symptoms: nullable_comment_not_annotation may be a systemic pattern across other utility/parser classes
possible_false_positives: requireNextArg System.exit is in Main.java which is a CLI entry point; this pattern is common and acceptable there, though still worth documenting
